2.text: The title shifts the focus from single-model refinement to population-level search over many candidate models.

4.text: Lab 23 improved outputs; this lab improves the configuration itself. The object being optimized changes from data to model population.

5.text: The key insight: instead of improving one model's outputs, we improve the model population itself through evolutionary pressure.

6.text: Each variant has different hyperparameters -- this diversity is what gives evolution something to select from.

7.text: Selection and mutation repeat over generations, so improvement comes from many small inherited changes rather than one optimizer run.

9.text: PBT is the core algorithm here: train many models in parallel, periodically replacing the worst with mutated copies of the best.

10.text: PBT is the direct template: train a population briefly, rank by fitness, then let strong members seed the next generation.

11.text: PBT combines hyperparameter search with ongoing training, so good settings can prove themselves under learning dynamics rather than static scoring.

12.text: Online selection is what distinguishes PBT from a static hyperparameter sweep: weak configs are replaced while training is still underway.

13.text: The population is not just ranked once. Replacement happens generation after generation, so search and learning are intertwined.

15.text: These related systems matter because they show the same outer-loop idea can optimize many kinds of candidates, not only neural net hyperparameters.

17.text: FunSearch is included as a conceptual cousin: both systems use an evolutionary outer loop to search over candidates scored by an objective.

18.text: Autoresearch provides the same narrative in sequential form: propose a change, measure it, and keep only the improvements.

22.text: No LLM needed for mutations -- simple random perturbations of numeric hyperparameters are enough for effective search.

23.text: The search space is intentionally small and interpretable, so each evolved improvement can be traced back to a concrete hyperparameter choice.

24.text: Validation loss is our fitness function -- evolution will minimize it by discovering good hyperparameter combinations.

25.text: That is the motivation for population search: a good region of configuration space can be found by repeated selection even when a single draw would likely miss it.

27.text: The docstring is explicit about scope: this lab teaches the exploit-explore control loop, not every engineering detail of production PBT.

28.text: Canonical PBT would overwrite weak population members in place; this lab keeps the same idea but expresses it with simpler rebuild-or-inherit logic.

29.text: Optimizer-state carryover is omitted here for simplicity, which makes inheritance easier to explain but slightly less faithful to industrial PBT.

30.text: Discrete choices keep the search space small and legible. You can point to every possible architectural value the loop is allowed to explore.

31.text: Fresh optimizers per generation keep the implementation small, even though production PBT would usually preserve optimizer momentum too.

34.text: Copying whole model states is part of the exploit step: strong survivors can seed the next generation.

35.text: Math is only needed for the usual transformer computations and reporting of loss values.

37.text: Fixed seeds ensure reproducibility -- every run produces the same evolutionary trajectory for fair comparison.

39.text: PyTorch provides the model and optimizer, while the evolutionary logic stays plain Python around it.

40.text: Modules still matter because architecture itself is part of the search space in this lab.

41.text: Functional helpers like cross-entropy keep the training loop compact so the evolutionary wrapper stays readable.

43.text: Fix seeds so the population trajectory is reproducible enough to compare baseline and evolved runs meaningfully.

49.text: Resolve the shared names corpus so every candidate model is judged on the same data distribution.

50.text: Fetch the dataset only if necessary. The experiment should be runnable from a clean checkout.

51.text: Shuffling before the train/val split prevents any ordering bias (e.g. alphabetical) from skewing results.

53.text: All population members train on the same underlying corpus. The only thing that changes is model configuration.

54.text: Auto-downloading keeps the experiment runnable from a fresh checkout, which matters because population methods already add enough moving parts on their own.

56.text: Every individual in the population ultimately trains on this same underlying corpus, which is what makes fitness comparisons meaningful.

57.text: Shuffle once before splitting so the held-out set is not a biased prefix of the alphabetically ordered names file.

58.text: Corpus size is printed up front so you can reason about how much data each generation is sampling from.

60.text: The vocabulary stays fixed across the whole population so models compete on architecture and optimization, not tokenization differences.

61.text: Character-to-ID mapping stays deterministic so every population member trains and validates on exactly the same tokenization.

62.text: Holding out 1000 names for validation is critical -- fitness must be measured on unseen data to avoid overfitting.

63.text: Vocabulary size is fixed before the search begins; evolution is not allowed to cheat by changing the token set.

64.text: Printing vocab size confirms that the evolutionary search is not changing the task itself, only the model and optimizer configuration.

66.text: A held-out split is essential because evolution needs a fitness signal that rewards generalization rather than memorized training loss.

67.text: Hold out 1,000 names so the fitness signal measures unseen-data performance.

68.text: Validation documents are kept separate from the search process so the population competes on unseen data.

69.text: block_size is fixed across the population -- it defines the maximum context window all models share.

70.text: Printing the split sizes makes the fitness budget explicit: one pool for training, one pool for selection.

75.text: Everything runs on CPU to keep the example local and deterministic. The evolutionary idea does not depend on accelerators.

76.text: The context window is fixed for the whole population so sequence length does not become another hidden variable.

79.text: RMSNorm stays unchanged from the earlier labs. Evolution is changing macro configuration, not swapping in new layer types.

80.text: RMSNorm remains parameter-free and unchanged; the search is over macro configuration, not normalization variants.

81.text: The normalization layer itself has no learned weights here, so widening the model changes surrounding matrices more than it changes normalization.

88.text: Attention now takes `n_embd` and `n_head` as parameters because those are evolvable traits in the search space.

89.text: Attention receives dynamic width and head-count arguments because those two architectural choices are part of the evolvable genome.

90.text: The attention module is rebuilt to match each sampled genome, which is why architectural mutation naturally changes parameter count too.

91.text: Store head count on the module because both tensor reshaping and legality checks depend on it.

93.text: Query projection width grows and shrinks with embedding size, so larger genomes pay for more expressive attention with more parameters.

98.text: The attention forward pass stays standard even though the model size changes. Evolution is selecting among architectures, not inventing new operators.

99.text: Unpack batch, time, and channel dimensions so the tensor geometry stays visible while the model size changes.

100.text: Queries are always projected at full head count. Evolution changes head count by rebuilding the module, not by changing the attention rule.

101.text: Keys follow the same head geometry as queries because this lab changes capacity through width and depth, not by introducing KV-sharing tricks.

102.text: Values mirror keys so attention remains standard scaled dot-product attention regardless of which genome is active.

103.text: Scaled dot-product attention is still standard. Evolution is not discovering a new attention rule here.

104.text: The causal mask is unchanged across the population. Evolution compares model sizes and learning rates, not different generation rules.

111.text: Blocks become parameterized because architecture depth and width are now part of the genome, not compile-time constants.

114.text: Each block carries the sampled width and head count through both attention and MLP, so a genome affects the full transformer stack.

117.text: The MLP expansion still uses the usual 4x multiplier. Capacity changes through `n_embd` and `n_layer`, not an extra search dimension.

118.text: The second linear layer contracts the 4x hidden expansion back to model width, making parameter count very sensitive to the sampled embedding dimension.

120.text: MicroGPT accepts n_embd, n_head, and n_layer as constructor arguments, making the architecture evolvable.

126.text: The top-level model constructor exposes the exact architectural knobs that evolution is allowed to search over.

127.text: n_layer controls depth -- evolution can discover whether 1 or 2 layers works best for this task.

128.text: The full model is assembled from the genome here: embeddings, transformer depth, and output head all depend on the sampled configuration.

129.text: Embedding tables scale with `n_embd`, so wider models immediately cost more parameters.

132.text: Small init std (0.08) keeps initial outputs low-entropy -- important so all population members start trainable.

136.text: Token and position embeddings are summed then normalized -- this is the model's input pipeline.

142.text: Sequence length is still inferred at runtime, so genomes change capacity without changing the caller interface.

143.text: Position embeddings still use the same fixed context length. This keeps comparisons between candidates fair.

149.text: Number of attention heads: 1, 2, or 4. Must evenly divide n_embd, which the code enforces.

150.text: Depth is limited to 1 or 2 layers -- even this binary choice has a big impact on model capacity vs. training speed.

151.text: Learning rate varies by 4x across the space -- this is often the most impactful hyperparameter to tune.

153.text: This discrete table is the search landscape. Evolution can only step among these predefined choices.

154.text: The search space is deliberately discrete and small so the reader can reason about every possible mutation.

155.text: Embedding size is the main capacity knob. Larger values increase both model expressiveness and parameter count.

156.text: random_config() is the initialization operator: it creates a single random individual for the population.

157.text: Depth is part of the genome too, which lets the search compare shallow and slightly deeper tiny GPTs under the same budget.

158.text: Each hyperparameter is sampled independently and uniformly from its allowed values.

159.text: Beta1 controls momentum in Adam -- lower values make the optimizer more responsive to recent gradients.

160.text: Constraint repair: if n_embd isn't divisible by n_head, resample n_head until the constraint is satisfied.

163.text: Random initialization samples a fresh genome for a new individual in the population.

164.text: A fresh genome is just a dict sampled from the search space; evolution later decides which of these simple objects deserve to survive.

165.text: A configuration is one genome in the population: architecture depth/width and optimizer settings all become evolvable traits.

166.text: This repair step prevents illegal genomes from surviving sampling or mutation, which keeps the search focused on runnable candidates.

167.text: This legality check prevents the search from proposing invalid head layouts where embedding width cannot be split evenly across heads.

168.text: Usually mutate 1 hyperparameter, sometimes 2. Small mutations keep children close to proven parents.

169.text: random.sample picks which hyperparameters to change -- any combination is possible.

172.text: Mutation starts from an existing survivor genome rather than from scratch, which is why the search can improve cumulatively.

173.text: Same constraint repair as in random_config -- mutations must also produce valid architectures.

174.text: Mutation starts by copying the parent genome so only the selected fields change and the rest of the inherited design stays intact.

175.text: Most children mutate only one trait, which keeps the search local enough for successful parents to pass on useful structure.

176.text: Sampling mutation keys without replacement means one generation cannot accidentally mutate the same hyperparameter twice in opposite directions.

177.text: Mutation is plain resampling from the discrete search space. There is no gradient for these choices, only selection pressure.

178.text: build_model() is a factory: given a config dict, it returns a fresh MicroGPT with those hyperparameters.

180.text: After mutation, the same divisibility guard runs again so children stay architecturally valid.

181.text: Repair after mutation matters most when width changed, because that can invalidate the current head-count choice.

185.text: train_model() runs a fixed number of gradient steps -- in PBT, all members train for the same number of steps per generation.

186.text: Model construction is factored out because evolution repeatedly instantiates architectures from sampled genomes.

187.text: Genome-to-model translation is a separate step so survivors can be copied, mutated, and rebuilt cleanly.

190.text: Training is intentionally short and fixed-length. Evolution needs many comparable partial runs, not one full convergence run.

191.text: Training budget is intentionally fixed per call. In this lab, compute allocation is equal across genomes so selection pressure stays fair.

192.text: Optimizer settings are part of the genome too, so learning dynamics themselves are under evolutionary control.

193.text: Adam hyperparameters come directly from the genome, which makes optimization dynamics part of what evolution is selecting for.

195.text: Average training loss is tracked per member mainly for observability; survival itself is still based on validation loss.

196.text: Cross-entropy loss is the training signal -- each member independently minimizes its own loss.

197.text: Each member trains on the same corpus slice pattern. Fairness comes from equal training budget per generation, not from different data.

198.text: Like the self-improving lab, names are still encoded as BOS-delimited character sequences before training.

200.text: Inputs stay on CPU here so population search can run in a minimal environment without distributed orchestration.

201.text: Returning average loss lets us monitor training quality, though fitness is measured separately on validation data.

203.text: Training loss drives weight updates inside a generation, but it is not the survival criterion. That distinction matters.

204.text: @torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking during evaluation -- we only need forward passes for fitness.

205.text: evaluate_model() computes the fitness function: validation loss on held-out data. Lower is better.

209.text: Capping at max_samples=200 speeds up evaluation -- we don't need the full val set to rank members.

214.text: Validation loss is the fitness signal. Lower is better, so evolution selects candidates that generalize best.

215.text: Validation loss is the fitness function. Lower loss means a genome earns the right to survive and reproduce.

218.text: Cap evaluation samples to keep selection cheap. The goal is a stable ranking signal, not a perfect benchmark.

220.text: Average validation loss is the fitness score. The evolution loop will sort the population by this value.

223.text: Evaluation reuses the same autoregressive objective, but on held-out names so the search favors generalizing configurations.

224.text: generate_names() lets us qualitatively inspect what each model has learned -- a sanity check beyond loss numbers.

232.text: Sampling from a model after evolution is not part of fitness, but it helps verify that lower validation loss corresponds to plausible-looking names.

233.text: Autoregressive generation: predict one token at a time, append it, and feed the growing sequence back in.

234.text: Temperature controls randomness: 0.5 is fairly conservative, producing more typical (less surprising) names.

235.text: BOS token acts as both start and end marker -- generating BOS means the name is complete.

237.text: Each sample starts from BOS, keeping generation behavior aligned with how the models were trained.

240.text: Generation is only for qualitative inspection. Fitness is decided numerically by validation loss, not by cherry-picked samples.

241.text: Scale logits by temperature before sampling so sample diversity can be inspected without changing the learned model.

243.text: Multinomial sampling exposes the actual learned distribution of the winner instead of forcing greedy outputs.

253.text: Counting parameters shows model capacity -- evolution might discover that smaller or larger models work better.

255.text: Phase 1 establishes a baseline: one randomly configured model trained for 500 steps. Evolution must beat this.

256.text: 500 training steps for the baseline gives it a fair amount of learning before we compare.

257.text: baseline_val_loss is the number to beat -- if evolution can't improve on a random config, it isn't working.

259.text: Start with one random baseline so the evolutionary run has a fair point of comparison instead of claiming improvement in the abstract.

261.text: Instantiate the baseline from the sampled config so later improvement can be measured against a single-run search.

265.text: Give the baseline a longer warm-up than each evolutionary mini-run so the comparison is not stacked against it.

267.text: The baseline validation score becomes the denominator for the final improvement claim: evolution should beat this single random trial.

268.text: POP_SIZE=8 is small but enough to demonstrate selection pressure. Production PBT uses dozens to hundreds.

270.text: STEPS_PER_GEN=200 means each member trains for 200 steps before being evaluated. Total: 200 x 6 = 1200 steps for survivors.

271.text: TOP_K=3 means the best 3 survive each generation. The other 5 are replaced by mutated offspring.

275.text: Population initialization: create POP_SIZE individuals, each with a random config and fresh model.

277.text: Population size controls how much diversity the search can maintain at once.

278.text: val_loss starts at infinity -- every member must earn its fitness through actual training and evaluation.

279.text: Each generation only gets 200 training steps. That is enough to rank candidates without making the demo too slow.

282.text: This is the initial population: multiple independently sampled genomes enter the search before any selection has happened.

283.text: The population container holds each individual’s identity, genome, model weights, and most recent fitness.

287.text: New members begin with infinite validation loss so they cannot be mistaken for evaluated survivors before training.

289.text: Population size determines search breadth: more individuals mean more exploration each generation but higher total compute cost.

290.text: Training continues from where the member left off -- survivors accumulate learning across generations.

291.text: After training, measure fitness. This val_loss determines who survives and who gets replaced.

294.text: Generation stats accumulate a compressed history of the search so the final report can show trend, not just the winner.

296.text: Each generation repeats the same cycle: train, evaluate, sort, keep the best, and repopulate.

297.text: Within a generation, every member gets trained before any survivor decision is made. Selection is based on a synchronized snapshot of the population.

298.text: Average loss across the population shows overall population fitness, not just the best individual.

299.text: Every generation gives each member the same training budget. That keeps the competition about configuration quality rather than extra compute.

300.text: Fitness is updated immediately after training so ranking reflects the current generation, not stale scores from earlier rounds.

302.text: Sorting by validation loss makes the best candidate land at index 0 and the worst at the end.

303.text: Sorting by validation loss creates the selection pressure. Once ordered, survivors and replacees are obvious.

305.text: Once sorted, `best` is both the current champion and the most likely parent source for the next generation.

307.text: Average population loss matters because evolution can improve the frontier while still collapsing diversity, and this metric helps reveal that.

309.text: Record generation-level telemetry so the search can be inspected as a trend in best, average, and worst population quality.

320.text: The per-generation printout exposes the whole exploit-explore story in one line: who won, how bad the tail is, and what genome led the field.

321.text: Survivors keep their trained weights -- this is the 'exploit' part of PBT. Good solutions are preserved.

323.text: We need POP_SIZE - TOP_K new children to fill the population back to its original size.

325.text: Random parent selection from survivors: each child descends from one of the top-K. This spreads good genes.

326.text: mutate_config creates the child's config by randomly perturbing 1-2 hyperparameters of the parent.

328.text: The evolutionary step starts here. Survivors stay; everyone else is replaced by mutated descendants.

329.text: The top-K slice is the survivor pool. These members are the only ones allowed to pass structure into the next generation.

330.text: Deep-copy the survivors so their trained weights persist into the next generation instead of being overwritten in place.

332.text: Exactly `POP_SIZE - TOP_K` children are created so population size stays constant across generations.

333.text: Parents are chosen from survivors only, which means every child descends from a genome that already proved itself on validation loss.

335.text: Each child starts from a successful parent genome, then explores nearby configurations by mutation.

340.text: load_state_dict copies all parameters -- the child starts where the parent left off, a huge head start.

342.text: Each child gets a unique ID for tracking lineage across generations.

344.text: This branch cleanly separates exploit from explore: exploit if weights are reusable, explore from scratch if the architecture changed too much.

345.text: If shape changed, exploration wins completely and the child starts from fresh random weights.

347.text: If the architecture matches, load the parent weights into the child. This is the exploit half of exploit/explore.

349.text: When shape is unchanged, the child inherits learned weights. This is the exploit step that makes PBT more efficient than restarting every trial.

351.text: Each child re-enters the population with a new identity and an unknown fitness, even if it inherited strong weights.

360.text: best_evolved is the fittest individual discovered across the entire evolutionary run.

365.text: Generation-by-generation table shows whether evolution is making progress -- best loss should decrease over time.

367.text: This final pass avoids a subtle reporting bug: the last batch of spawned children has been created but not yet trained unless you evaluate them here.

374.text: Sort again after the final training pass so the reported champion is the best model in the fully evaluated last population.

375.text: Positive improvement means evolution beat the baseline -- the search found a better region of hyperparameter space.

378.text: The generation summary table makes it easy to see whether evolution is steadily improving best loss or merely reshuffling the population.

380.text: The summary table gives a generation-by-generation view of search progress, which is the clearest way to see whether evolution is genuinely converging.

387.text: This is the headline question: did evolutionary search beat a single random configuration on the same task?

389.text: The baseline-vs-evolved comparison is the answer to the lab’s main question: did population search beat a single random guess?

391.text: Printing each member's config reveals how much the population has converged -- similar configs suggest a fitness peak.

393.text: Side-by-side samples keep the numerical improvement grounded in generated outputs, not just in lower validation loss.

398.text: Sample from the evolved winner to make the improvement visible beyond the validation metric.

403.text: Listing the whole final population shows whether multiple genome families survived or one configuration dominated everything.

405.text: Printing the whole final population exposes diversity, showing whether evolution converged tightly or kept multiple viable regions alive.

408.text: The PBT loop in summary: initialize, train, evaluate, select, mutate, inherit, repeat.

418.text: Lab 23 improves training data (output quality); Lab 24 improves the model architecture and optimizer settings.

420.text: Lab 23 is like self-play refinement (STaR); Lab 24 is like natural selection -- fundamentally different strategies.

422.text: The numbered recap restates the algorithm as a control loop: initialize, train, evaluate, select, mutate, inherit, repeat.

424.text: EXPLOIT: children inherit trained weights from successful parents, getting a head start on learning.

425.text: EXPLORE: random hyperparameter mutations ensure the population doesn't get stuck in a local optimum.

428.text: PBT was originally designed to evolve learning rate schedules and data augmentation during training.

430.text: FunSearch uses the same pattern but evolves LLM-generated programs instead of numeric hyperparameters.

438.text: Exploit means reusing what already works; explore means mutating it enough to discover a better region of the search space.

448.text: The closing claim is intentionally modest: this is a tiny illustrative search, but it shares the same control structure as large-scale tuning systems.
